title: Path-Relaxed Reasoning Optimization for Large Reasoning Models: A Travelling Salesman Problem Relaxation Framework for Structured Inference Training (PRRO-LRM)
author: Zhang, Jincheng
license_note: Zenodo record metadata license field: cc-by-4.0
description: Large reasoning models (LRMs) typically enhance reasoning capabilities in complex tasks by generating long-chain thought processes. However, this "step-by-step unfolding reasoning" is essentially similar to finding a feasible path in an exponentially large state space, resulting in high computational costs and numerous redundant steps. Existing methods often suppress redundant reasoning through truncation, distillation, or external control, neglecting the combinatorial optimization structure of the reasoning path itself. This paper proposes a novel training paradigm: Path-Relaxed Reasoning Optimization (PRRO). This method introduces the relaxation concept from the Traveling Salesman Problem (TSP) in classical combinatorial optimization into reasoning trajectory modeling. It treats discrete reasoning steps as "reasoning paths" that access semantic state nodes and, through continuous relaxation and dual constraint learning, optimizes "fractional reasoning paths" rather than hard discrete chains during the training phase. This significantly reduces the length of redundant reasoning while maintaining correctness.

Existing methods often suppress redundant reasoning through truncation, distillation, or external control, neglecting the combinatorial optimization structure of the reasoning path itself. This paper proposes a novel training paradigm: Path-Relaxed Reasoning Optimization (PRRO). This method introduces the relaxation concept from the Traveling Salesman Problem (TSP) in classical combinatorial optimization into reasoning trajectory modeling. It treats discrete reasoning steps as "reasoning paths" that access semantic state nodes and, through continuous relaxation and dual constraint learning, optimizes "fractional reasoning paths" rather than hard discrete chains during the training phase. This significantly reduces the length of redundant reasoning while maintaining correctness.
